g.data-narration: Welcome to Virtual Memory Management - a fundamental concept in modern operating systems that enables efficient memory utilization.
Virtual memory creates an illusion of larger contiguous memory by mapping virtual addresses to physical memory frames using page tables.
g.text: Abstraction layer between logical (process) and physical memory
Enables processes to use more memory than physically available
Uses paging: divide virtual/physical memory into fixed-size pages/frames

g.data-narration: Virtual memory provides memory isolation between processes, allows memory overcommitment, and simplifies programming with contiguous addresses.
Demand paging loads pages into memory only when needed, marked by a valid-invalid bit in the page table entry.
g.text: Load pages into memory only when accessed (on page fault)
Uses valid-invalid bit in page table: Valid=in memory, Invalid=not loaded

g.data-narration: When a page fault occurs, the OS finds a free frame, loads the required page from disk, updates the page table, and restarts the instruction.
g.text: 6. Restart Instruction - Jump back to faulting instruction with page now in memory

g.data-narration: The Effective Access Time formula calculates average memory access time considering page fault rate and page fault service overhead.
g.text: p = page fault rate (0 ≤ p ≤ 1), ma = memory access time

g.data-narration: Copy-on-Write optimizes process creation by sharing pages until modification, crucial for efficient fork() and memory sharing.
g.text: Parent and child share pages initially; copies made only on modification
Essential for efficient fork() in Unix/Linux systems
2. Write → Page fault → Copy page → Remap

g.data-narration: When no free frames exist, page replacement algorithms decide which victim page to evict. Key algorithms include FIFO, Optimal, and LRU.
g.text: Used when free frames unavailable - selects victim page to evict

g.data-narration: FIFO replaces the oldest page in memory using a queue structure. It suffers from Belady's Anomaly where more frames can cause more faults.
g.text: Uses queue structure: Enqueue on load, Dequeue on replacement
Suffers from Belady's Anomaly - more frames can increase page faults!

g.data-narration: Optimal algorithm replaces the page not used for the longest time in the future - it's theoretical but serves as the perfect benchmark.
g.text: Replace page that will not be used for the longest time in the future
Theoretical algorithm - requires future knowledge; used as benchmark

g.data-narration: LRU approximates optimal by replacing the least recently used page. Implementation uses counters (timestamps) or stack tracking.
g.text: Replace page that hasn't been used for the longest time - approximates Optimal
Implementation: Counter-based (timestamp each ref) or Stack (move accessed page to top)
Total Faults = 10 (better than FIFO, worse than OPT)

g.data-narration: Clock (Second Chance) uses a circular queue with reference bits. If R=1, give second chance; if R=0, replace the page.
g.text: Circular queue with reference (R) bit per page
If R=1: Clear bit, give second chance | If R=0: Replace page
Pages in memory have R=1 (recently used)
Scan circular buffer: Skip R=1 (set to 0), replace first R=0

g.data-narration: Belady's Anomaly is the counterintuitive phenomenon where increasing memory frames can increase page faults - only FIFO exhibits this.
g.text: Counterintuitive: More frames can lead to MORE page faults!
Only FIFO exhibits this anomaly - OPT and LRU never have this problem

g.data-narration: Thrashing occurs when the system spends more time paging than executing due to overcommitted memory, causing high page fault rates.
g.text: Excessive paging activity - CPU spends more time swapping pages than executing
Causes: Over-commitment of memory, low CPU utilization, high page fault rate
2. Page fault → steal frames from other processes
3. Those processes fault → steal more → cascade

g.data-narration: Solutions to thrashing include the Working Set Model, Page Fault Frequency control, and limiting multiprogramming level.
Remember the EAT formula and Belady's Anomaly concept - these are frequently tested in exams with numerical problems.
g.text: Only FIFO exhibits: more frames → more faults possible

g.data-narration: Virtual memory enables efficient memory utilization through demand paging and page replacement. Master FIFO, LRU, and the EAT formula for exams.
g.text: • Master FIFO, LRU, Optimal, and CLOCK algorithms
• Practice numericals: Page replacement sequences, EAT calculations
• Remember: Only FIFO has Belady's Anomaly
• Know COW for fork() and Working Set for thrashing
Virtual Memory: The foundation of modern OS memory management
